[IMAGE:0]It's getting on for midnight, so we can proceed with a new Magic Monday. Ask me anything about occultism and I'll do my best to answer it. With certain exceptions, any question received by midnight Monday Eastern time will get an answer. Please note:  Any question received after then will not get an answer, and in fact will just be deleted. I've been getting an increasing number of people trying to post after these are closed, so will have to draw a harder line than before.) If you're in a hurry, or suspect you may be the 143,916th person to ask a question, please check out the very rough version 1.0 of The Magic Monday FAQ here. Also: I will not be putting through or answering any more questions about practicing magic around children. I've answered those in simple declarative sentences in the FAQ. If you read the FAQ and don't think your question has been answered, read it again. If that doesn't help, consider remedial reading classes; yes, it really is as simple and straightforward as the FAQ says. 

The image? I've decided to trace, as far as I can, my own occult lineage in photos. We're still tracing Juliet Ashley's end of the lineage. This is her fourth teacher, Arthur Edward Waite, who passed onto her the rituals and lineage that became the Fellowship of the Hermetic Rose I've been posting about for a while now. Waite was an odd duck, a devout if eccentric Christian from an impoverished background who clawed his way up to the middle class via a writing career.  He was passionately interested in occultism; he joined the Hermetic Order of the Golden Dawn, and when it blew itself apart in a series of political squabbles in 1900-1903, he took a faction of Christian members with him and founded his own version of the Order with no ceremonial magic and a lot of Christian mysticism. (The rituals are publicly available now, and out of copyright; you can find them here.) Evelyn Underhill, the famous writer on Christian mysticism, was one of his order's members; Charles Williams, who wrote Christian occult thrillers, was another; and of course Pamela Coleman Smith, creator of the Rider-Waite tarot deck, was active as well.

If you're interested in political and economic astrology, or simply prefer to use a subscription service to support your favorite authors, you can find my Patreon page here and my SubscribeStar page here.

Since my ADHD diagnosis, and, well, the somewhat rapid climb in interest in the condition, I've been wondering about the occult origins of it, as in, what is the karmic cause of it? I've read various theories, ranging from the Hindu theory that ADHD folks are old souls, near the end of their human journey, through to western clairvoyant perceptions of seeing a dark energy band in the aura (just in front of the forehead).

I'm reluctant to buy into the disability as special sauce theories, I think you have said elsewhere that disabilities are just that, and not to be over analyzed.

Still, the karmic origins of ADHD interest me. Is it a conscious choice, to have neurobiology that produces less executive function than neurotypicals, or is it a matter of advanced soils not fully incarnation into matter and being caught with some of their mind still up on the astral?

Insights gratefully received

Having something of an inward looking tendency, I liked this piece I read recently and thought I'd offer a rough translation up as a piece for meditating on. I'd also be interested in hearing your thoughts on the claim that humanity's primary problem is depending upon others and not taking a proper look at themselves. The piece is from the concluding summary of:
Kernning, J. [Johann Baptiste Krebs] Wegen zur Unsterblichkeit auf unläugbare Kräfte der menschlichen Natur gegründet [Paths to immortality based on undeniable powers of human nature] (18??) Schwäbisch Hall und Leipzig: F. F. Haspel’schen Buchhandlung.
The translation is mine (original follows for those who can read German - note that spirit is an imperfect translation for Geist, a word which gives translators into English headaches):

The aim of all life: seek the spirit in yourself, then you are safe. But seek your spirit, not another's. Here lies a primary problem with humanity, that they always depend on others and never give themselves a proper look. The spirit of another never becomes mine, it can merely illuminate me, even coming into my temple, but as little as I can implant and use the arm of another as my own, just as little do I allow the powers of the spirit to be enclosed in other forms. With water you can tune glasses to produce a chord; take the water out, pour it in other glasses, then you have no chord and even none of the previous notes anymore. Everything you wish and desire must come from yourself; if you know this, learn it; rid yourself of all that is foreign, seek your ego, never the ego of another, only then is it possible to obtain the infallibility of life.
Harmony of the tones and mathematical forms are based on infallible principles and therefore cannot deceive; the harmony of your life must become as clear, which like the former is based on unalterable laws and bears its infallibility within itself.
[Ziel alles Lebens: suche den Geist in dir, dann bist du in Sicherheit. Aber deinen Geist suche, keinen andern. Hier liegt ein Grundübel der Menschen, daß sie sich immer nach Andern richten und nie sich selber gehörig betrachten. Der Geist eines Andern wird niemals mein, er kann mir bloß leuchten, auch in meinen Tempel zu kommen, aber so wenig ich den Arm eines Andern als den meinigen einsetzen und gebrauchen kann, eben so wenig lass ich die Kräfte des Geistes in andere Formen einschließen. Mit Wasser kann man die Gläser stimmen, daß sie einen Akkord geben; nimm das Wasser heraus, gieße es in andere Gläser, so hast du keinen Akkord und sogar keinen von den vorigen [169|170] Tönen mehr. Aus dir muß alles kommen, was du wünschest und begehrst; wenn du dieses noch kannst, so lerne es; mache dich los von allem Fremden, suche dein Ich, nie das Ich eines Andern, dann nur ist es möglich, zur Unfehlbarkeit des Lebens zu gelangen.
Harmonie der Töne und mathematische Formen beruhen auf unfehlbaren Grundsätzen und können daher nicht täuschen; so klar muß dir die Harmonie des Lebens werden, welche wie jene auf unabänderliche Gesetze gegründet ist und die Unfehlbarkeit in sich selber trägt.]

replies.content:
On 4) - Not something I can directly attest to, but something I've heard multiple credible reports on. Of course, this is in no way medical advice and you should consult with appropriately licensed folks in your jurisdiction, and all of that:

Very many folks (but not all) report higher levels of energy and/or improvement to fatigue when eating a ketogenic diet. I've especially heard this for folks who took a heavy course of antibiotics to treat Lyme Disease and have had lingering fatigue/brain fog.

What a Ketogenic Diet Is: You eat foods high in fat and very very few carbohydrates, along with moderate amounts of protein. The goal is to get your body into a state called "ketosis" (not to be confused with "ketoacidosis", which can happen to diabetics and is very dangerous), where your liver starts converting fats into "ketones", which are available to your nervous system as energy the way carbohydrates are, but normal fats are not. You can also get into ketosis via fasting, but obviously that's riskier and you can't do it for as long as you can maintain dietary ketosis. Ketosis is why folks don't starve to death after a couple of days of not eating - your body can only store about 2-3 days worth of glucose, but it can store rather a lot more fat that can be turned into ketones.

Purported Benefits: Some folks report that when they're in ketosis, they have higher levels of energy, improved cognitive function, and elevated mood. Many folks also report weight loss when following a ketogenic diet, and it's popular among endurance athletes, as your body is already in "fat-burning" mode rather than having to burn through all the glucose first. It was also originally developed for folks with epilepsy, and apparently having ketones in the blood is protective against all kinds of seizures.

Important Caveats: Some folks don't respond as well to ketogenic diets as others - for example, when I was consistently in dietary ketosis for months at a time, I noticed that tiredness didn't bother me as much, but I never felt the kind of mental clarity and consistently elevated mood that some folks report. A later genetic test indicated my body might be mediocre at ketosis. As our host has pointed out in the past, diet is very individualized, so pay attention to what feels good for you! Some folks also have other negative reactions, like poor blood pressure or headaches or the like. Also, staying in ketosis is a major pain in the keister - you have to be very careful about what you eat, and going out of and back into ketosis any more regularly than every other week or so is not very pleasant (I tried "cycling out" every weekend to have a day where I could enjoy non keto-friendly foods and I ended up feeling like crap all the time).

At any rate, not the right answer for everyone, but maybe something to consider. Good luck with the fatigue!
Jeff

Still, just sitting in the sun is the big one. The rest is auxiliary. Works for me. No idea if it works for others, and I'm not making any medical recommendations here.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Geomancy is easy unless you overthink it, and it sounds as though you're overthinking it. To get out of that trap, ignore the rest of the reading, take the judge and witnesses, and interpret those as an answer. The witnesses are both negative figures, i.e., it's a bad idea. The judge is positive, i.e., there won't be any long-term consequences. You can figure out what kind of a bad idea it is by reading the first witness as what happens to you, and the second witness as what happens to the situation. (Hint: the money will be wasted and nothing will happen as a result.) Since Fortuna Major is the judge, what you need to do is just keep on doing what you're doing and be patient, and it will work out well in the longer run.

replies.title: Re: Occult Philosophy Workbook vs. Circles of Power
replies.content:
There's no overlap to speak of, and they're also from completely different systems of occult practice. Circles of Power is a practical manual of Golden Dawn ritual magic, while The Occult Philosophy Workbook is a one-year course of meditations and other spiritual (not magical) practices.

If you're interested in Golden Dawn magic, instead of The Occult Philosophy Workbook, I'd encourage you to get Paths of Wisdom, which covers the core occult theory of the GD system and is intended to go with Circles of Power. If the meditation and philosophical material in The Occult Philosophy Workbook is what interests you, a better choice to go along with it would be The Way of the Golden Section, which is the practical manual that goes with it, and The Sacred Geometry Oracle, which provides the core symbolic system of the Golden Section path.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Welcome to the world of Christian Druidry. There's a lot of it, going back to pioneering 18th-century Druid Rev. William Stukeley, an Anglican minister; quite a bit of the older Druid literature has that focus. To make a start, I'd encourage you to reread the first chapter of the Gospel according to John and reflect on what it means that Christ, as the Logos, "made all things, and without Him was not anything made that was made." Oh, and what it means -- with an eye toward nature -- to say that "In Him was life, and the life was the light of men."

I don't happen to know what's available on the internet or in recent books on the subject, but you might be interested in looking through The Barddas of Iolo Morganwg sometime; his theology was quite strictly monotheistic. As for the eight festivals, check your traditional Christian calendar and see just how many of them have close equivalents there. Hmm -- Candlemas, the feast of the Annunciation, Lady Day, St. John's day...

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Hi Damian, I have been celebrating the eight seasonal festivals as a Christian Druid for about six years now, so hopefully my perspective is helpful. First of all, the various saints' days are, for better or worse, closely linked with their seasonality through two thousand years of European culture, if nothing else - so you might consider giving them a try. St. Peter, for instance, is a near-perfect match for All Saint's Day, if you think of his traditional role guarding the gates of the afterlife. St. John has a long association with the height of summer (St. John's Wort blooms near the solstice, for instance, and is named accordingly), and while you can find countless people online arguing over whether St. Brigid is the "same" as the goddess Brigid, they both have enough correspondences to Candlemas, and sacred fires, that you should have no problem there.

That said, just as you can do the Sphere of Protection with a purely elemental approach rather than bringing deities into it, you can also just do eight seasonal festivals to celebrate Nature directly and get a lot of mileage out of it. The changing cycles of light and dark, planting and harvest, warmth and cold, and the fact that those three cycles don't all line up perfectly - you can get a lifetime worth of meditation out of it. Christians have long associated the cycle of saints' days with seasonal celebrations - the Old Order Mennonites near me still roast the traditional goose at Michealmas - and you might find yourself happier not trying to force Christianity to fit the neopagan calendar but "leaning in" to eight saints' days that can easily accommodate celebrations of nature as well.

replies.title: Re: Sports astrology
replies.content:
In the old Horoscope A to Z by Llewellyn George (editions before the 1970s), there is a chapter relating to sports astrology. Llewellyn George was an avid sports fan, and he compiled into that chapter every scrap of astrology related to predicting the outcome of contests of all kinds. The "New A to Z" scrapped that chapter, so it is best to get a used older edition or one of the repop facsmiles.

The method involved making a horoscope reading at the moment the contest started, and assigning particular roles to the various houses, and predicting the outcome based on relative strengths and weaknesses of the planetary rulers of said houses.

As it is a question of judging contests, it could also be used for political contests, and if my faulty memory of 2020 could be trusted, it provided an accurate reading that the results of that political contest would be quite muddled.

As always, thanks to our host, and thanks to the community as well, for your generosity and knowledge.

To Share: Not exactly occult, but possibly helpful for anyone who takes advantage of public domain .pdfs but would rather have paper copies of books. There's a useful piece of software called "Bookbinder" that you can find here: http://quantumelephant.co.uk/bookbinder/bookbinder.html You use it to open the .pdf you'd like to turn into a printed book, fiddle with a few settings (the documentation here helps: http://www.quantumelephant.co.uk/bookbinder/docs/bbdocs.html), and it creates a set of files for "signatures" (the clumps of folded pages bound together to make a book). Works easiest if you have access to a duplex (two-sided) printer, but with a bit more work can work with a normal one-sided printer. Of course, you then have to fold all the pages and either sew or glue them together with a cover, but if you do, you have a real, live book! I've got Steiner's The Way of Initiation and Yeats's A Vision printed out and awaiting some time for binding.

comments.title: Magical Overshoot
comments.content:
I've happened upon a correlation in your multiple fields of study and work; I believe my realization may be of help to someone(s) in the commentariat (many of whom have helped me with their posts and questions.)

A civilization fails because it cannot pay the maintenance costs of it's overgrown infrastructure.

I've noticed this happens with individuals as well, but on much shorter timescales, and often multiple times in a single life.
I've noted too that we are encouraged (through credit and other means) to rapidly acquire as much as we can possibly possess with no thought to the maintenance of those items; making these minor collapses even more prevalent.

I naively followed this same pattern magically. I started with Sphere of Protection work from the DMH, but quickly added religious elements, acquired new books, started new practices. Before I knew it, I had a shelf of occult literature, a shoddy SOP practice, sporadic mediation, a year of rituals from three different paths, and little progress to show. It took years of following a path for a few months and moving to another with snail pace growth, before I narrowed focus and made some progress. This was actually inadvertently caused by moving, and having only one book to hand, the rest packed away.

Since focusing and really putting in the effort to a basic SOP and meditation, I've experienced leaps and bounds of improvement. I've been eager to start OSA work, but I've learned my lesson and know that I still lack enough will and energy (and time) to consistently pay the maintenance costs on my current practice. So I'm continuing on, and much more balanced and integrated than I've been in years.

So, my message to anyone struggling. Simplify to something you can truly achieve (not ideally, but realistically,) and achieve it before moving on or adding on.

(All this is said with the caveat that I read and heard this many times before learning it; but if one person needs to read it today - well, there you go.)

:)
Wishing you well, JMG. Thank you

comments.title: Calcination
comments.content:
Last week you mentioned having had good results doing calcination in a Webber grill. Could you offer a few pointers?
1) a charcoal grill? (I think there are also propane versions.)
2) is a pyrex dish adequate for the heat or should some sort of crucible be used?
3) in general, anything else?
(I found myself using up too much Everclear trying to calcinate with flame. Even after about four repetitions I wasn't getting a white ash. And Everclear gets expensive, even the 151 proof stuff I can buy here where I live; I haven't tried distilling wine yet, though it's tempting and I've assembled some of the necessary gear. Using my oven, as I mentioned last week, stunk up the house for several days; a good thing that I live alone! As an aside, violin makers seem to have great expertise in producing very pure ethanol; they use it as a varnish solvent.)

replies.title: Re: Calcination
replies.content: You can't calcine with alcohol. It doesn't burn hot enough. Charcoal does. I used a standard Weber kettle, charcoal, and a porcelain crucible -- Pyrex isn't durable enough. I'd get the charcoal going and then burn the herbal residue to black ash by lighting the alcohol on fire. When it went out, I'd pour in more alcohol and repeat.  Meanwhile the charcoal is going. I'd wait until the flames died down, then use the tongs to arrange the coals so that there was a little nest into which the crucible could fit directly, in contact with red-hot coals on all sides. The black ash from the herbs goes into the crucible, and is stirred with an old steel chopstick; over fifteen minutes or so of stirring, it burns and bubbles and turns into light gray or white ash. Victory! The crucible goes indoors to be put on a heatproof surface for a couple of hours until it and its contents are down to room temperature. Oh, and wear eye protection, heatproof gloves, and a fireproof apron the whole time.

comments.title: Memories
comments.content:
Hi JMG,

You may not have a good answer to this, but I'll ask anyway:

Why do we forget our memories between lives?

I know you've said before that we never actually lose the knowledge from previous lives, and it is 'uploaded' (for want of a better term) to our higher self, but it just seems so pointless having to trial-and-error every life over and over again.

It also seems like we don't just forget our previous lives, but literally everything about how to operate a human body.

The human gestation period is *relatively* short for our size, due to our disproportionately large skulls, but actually not by that much (a horse is about 40 days longer, and a gorilla is about 40 days less). But when most animals can at least stand unaided not long after being born, and horses can run only a few hours after birth, it does seem like we are born at a significant disadvantage, being unable to even hold our giant heads up for several months.

Is this just from us not having the evolutionary incentive to not have helpless babies (ie. humans can defend babies from predators fairly well), or is this some aspect of the Fall of Man (as you've touched on before), whereby the mythical tree of the knowledge of good and evil also took from us our animal instincts, so on each incarnation we have to re-learn how to move, etc. all over again?

And finally - is there any occult practice or school which offers the promise of being able to remember one's past lives? I don't mean in adulthood, but from birth or at least early childhood, and then to be able to retain that knowledge into adulthood? (As many children actually do remember snippets of past lives, but forget them as they get older).

Thanks,
Mr. Crow

replies.title: Re: Memories
replies.content:
There's a very good reason for it. Your memories are what hold your personality in place. As long as you remember the same things, your personality is relatively fixed, and that's a problem -- it puts hard limits on how much you can grow and develop. That's why it's necessary to face each life fresh, with a new personality and newly gathered memories, so you can break new ground in each life. It's a bad idea to force recollection of past lives; when you're nearly finished with material incarnation, they start to return all by themselves, and that's part of the process by which you realize your identity as your higher self.

Does it suck? Yes. I remember telling my mother, back when I was about five, "Childhood is such a waste." But it's part of the process of moving through this crucial phase of spiritual evolution.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Ahem. There's been a lot of, er, smoke shoveled about the Right and Left Hand Paths in recent years. The teachings I follow don't include either of those notions, and I have real doubts about whether they're even possible.

In the occult traditions I follow, there isn't an endgame. Souls are capable of immense growth and differentiation over aeons of cosmic time. We are already immortal; we are not gods, nor will we ever be -- there are eternal realms beyond created being where divinity dwells -- but we can become vastly greater, wiser, and more blissful than we can even imagine now.

Think of the difference between your consciousness and that of a single cell of blue green algae; there are beings in the cosmos that have risen further above us, in terms of richness and complexity of consciousness, than you are above that lively little bit of pond scum. Each of us has the capacity to rise to heights unimaginable to us today. As for our lives as human beings, that's just one incident in a vast trajectory. You have been many things before now, and so have I; you will be many more things after this life, and so will I; with that in mind, we can focus on making the most of the life we've got right now, and proceed from there.
